title: Arithmetic hyperbolic 3-manifolds, perfectoid spaces, and Galois representations II
speaker: Peter Scholze
abstract: One of the most studied objects in mathematics is the modular curve, which is the quotient of hyperbolic 2-space by the action of \(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb Z)\). It is naturally the home of modular forms, but it also admits an algebraic structure. The interplay of these structures lies at the heart of the Langlands correspondence for \(\mathrm{GL}_2/\mathbb Q\), connecting modular forms with Galois representations. The natural generalizations of the modular curve to higher dimension are the arithmetic locally symmetric spaces.

proof_stages.label: Bianchi-manifold special case
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes K = Q(i) and GL2, identifies the symmetric space with hyperbolic 3-space, and forms congruence quotients H^3/Gamma known as Bianchi manifolds or orbifolds.

proof_stages.label: Torsion-homology motivation
proof_stages.summary: The speaker stresses that, unlike modular curves, these odd-dimensional hyperbolic manifolds have no algebraic structure but do have large and poorly understood torsion in integral homology.

proof_stages.label: Rough mod-p Galois-representation expectation
proof_stages.summary: Nonzero mod-p homology of such congruence quotients is expected, and now known in the theorem being discussed, to give a mod-p Galois representation of the absolute Galois group of K unramified outside the level.

proof_stages.label: Hecke correspondences and precise theorem
proof_stages.summary: Using congruence subgroups, finite-covering correspondences define commuting Hecke operators, and a simultaneous Hecke eigenvalue system in mod-p homology is matched by the theorem with Frobenius traces of a Galois representation.

proof_stages.label: Concrete mod-3 eigensystem data
proof_stages.summary: A specific level Gamma1(3) intersect Gamma0(61) and Nebentypus character are chosen, giving a two-dimensional F3-homology eigenspace with two computed Hecke eigenvalue systems and visible numerical patterns.

proof_stages.label: SL2(F3) representations and cyclotomic twist
proof_stages.summary: The theorem attaches representations rho1 and rho2 into SL2(F3), and comparison with the mod-3 cyclotomic character explains the data by suggesting that rho2 is the cyclotomic twist of rho1.

proof_stages.label: Projective A4 field and root criterion
proof_stages.summary: Projectivizing rho1 gives an A4-valued representation corresponding to a quartic extension, and a lemma identifies nonzero Hecke trace with the associated quartic polynomial having a root modulo the prime.

proof_stages.label: Even Artin and Maass-form implications
proof_stages.summary: The same quartic polynomial is viewed as coming from an even Artin representation over Q, leading to a discussion of Maass forms, restriction to K, predicted torsion in Bianchi homology, and a possible route toward Galois representations for Maass forms.

examples.name: Even A4 Artin representation and a Maass-form test case
examples.note: Reinterpreting the same polynomial as an even Artin representation over Q leads to the proposed strategy that Maass forms might force Bianchi torsion and thereby yield Galois representations.
